name: Word Patterns — Spotting How French Works

cards.grammarNote: To form a -ment adverb, use the feminine adjective form: lent → lente → lentement. This is the standard rule for most adjectives.
heureux → heureuse → heureusement (fortunately / happily). The -eux → -euse feminine change is regular for this adjective group.

cards.front: évidemment — which rule does it follow?
cards.back: The -amment/-emment exception: évident ends in -ent, so it uses -emment
cards.grammarNote: Adjectives ending in -ant or -ent do not follow the feminine + -ment rule. Instead: -ant → -amment (courant → couramment), -ent → -emment (évident → évidemment). Both endings sound identical.

cards.front: fait is the past participle of ___
cards.grammarNote: faire → fait is one of the most important irregular past participles. 'Il a fait' = he did / he made. Also used as an adjective: un travail bien fait (a job well done).

cards.front: What are the past participles of avoir and être?
cards.grammarNote: These two are essential. eu (had): j'ai eu. été (been): j'ai été. Note that été is also the word for 'summer' — same spelling, different meaning in context.

cards.front: Give the past participles of: prendre, dire, voir
cards.back: prendre → pris / dire → dit / voir → vu
cards.grammarNote: Three key irregular participles ending in a consonant: pris (taken), dit (said), vu (seen). All use avoir as auxiliary in the passé composé.

cards.front: What does 'une porte fermée' mean, and why is it -ée?
cards.back: a closed door — fermée agrees with porte (feminine singular)
cards.grammarNote: Past participles used as adjectives must agree in gender and number: une porte fermée (f. sg.), des portes fermées (f. pl.), un magasin fermé (m. sg.).

cards.front: What gender are -tion / -sion nouns in French?
cards.back: All feminine — la communication, la situation, l'expression
cards.grammarNote: -tion and -sion nouns are always feminine in French. They are also almost always identical in meaning to their English counterparts — a huge ready-made vocabulary.

cards.front: How do you say 'singer' in French (male and female)?
cards.grammarNote: chanter (to sing) → chanteur / chanteuse. The -eur suffix marks a 'doer' noun. Feminine form: replace -eur with -euse.

cards.front: What is the feminine of directeur?
cards.grammarNote: Words ending in -teur often form the feminine with -trice instead of -teuse: directeur → directrice, acteur → actrice. This mirrors English 'actor → actress'.

cards.front: How would you say 'to start again' in French, using a prefix?
cards.grammarNote: The prefix re- means 'again' or 'back': relire (reread), refaire (redo), recommencer (restart). Identical in function to English re-.

cards.front: disparaître — what does the prefix tell you?
cards.back: dis- means 'opposite / undo': paraître (to appear) → disparaître (to disappear)
cards.grammarNote: dé- and dis- both reverse the meaning of a verb: habiller (to dress) → déshabiller (to undress), connecter → déconnecter. Mirrors English de-/dis- prefixes.
